titleStmt.title: The Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive

respStmt.name: University of South Carolina Center for Digital Humanities
The Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive Research and Development Team
University of South Carolina Center for Digital Humanities

availability.p: Periodicals and books published before 1 January 1923 are in the public domain. If re-publishing, quoting from, or reproducing images from this digital archive, we request that you cite it as follows: Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive, Center for Digital Humanities, University of South Carolina.

Periodicals and books published on or after 1 January 1923 may be protected by U.S. Copyright Law. All attempts to secure permission for republishing post-1923 material in this archive have been made. The names, logos, and identifying images, as well as the content of the articles, remain the property of the periodicals and/or of the authors and artists themselves. Further reproduction, as well as quotation beyond fair use, is prohibited; permission must be obtained from the copyright holder. Archival material published in this archive is used with permission of the institution or library that holds it. Further reproduction, as well as quotation beyond fair use, is prohibited; permission must be obtained from the copyright holder.
Responsibility for any use of the contents of this digital archive rests exclusively with the user.

bibl.title: Woman. Hermit of San Nicholas. Her Unbroken Solitude of Eighteen Years. Small Island in the Pacific Which Has Proved a Treasure House for the Archaeologists

head.title: Hermit of San Nicholas.
Her Unbroken Solitude of Eighteen Years.
Small Island in the Pacific Which Has Proved a Treasure House For the Archaeologists.

div.p: About 80 miles off the coast of Los Angeles to the southwest lies the Island of San Nicolas. Its shores are wind-swept and forbidding, it has neither tree nor shrub on its entire area, and its surface is largely composed of loose and shifting sands. The island is a tableland, 500 to 600 feet high, about nine miles in length, with an average width of about four miles.

San Nicolas has not always been as we now find it. At one time it had forests of trees and was densely populated. As late as 1853 Captain Nidever, of Santa Barbara, speaks of bushes. That it was densely populated is evidenced by the immense mounds of sea shells, principally abalone, which are yet found on the tableland of the interior. One row or rick of shells, as high as a man's head and almost a mile in length, must have required centuries to form.

p.span: But little is known of the character of the early inhabitants, although there are many persons still living who saw the last remnant of the race of Indians who formerly inhabited the island in the person of "The Lone Woman of San Nicolas."

div.p: Dr. Stephen Bowers, of Los Angeles, had the story of this woman, who for 18 years was the sole inhabitant of the island, directly from the lips of Captain John [sic] Nidever, who took her from the island to Santa Barbara in 1853.

In the early days the Russians made occasional visits to San Nicolas Island in quest of the valuable fur of the sea otter. On one occasion they brought with them natives from Alaska, who were left on the island to prosecute the work of capturing otter, while they returned to the North. While the Russians were absent, these Alaska Indians, having been taught the use of firearms, took possession of the women of the island and killed all the native men and male children. When the Russian ships came again they carried away the Alaskans back to their home and the

span.span: WOMEN WERE LEFT TO THEIR FATE.

div.p: Subsequently the mission fathers on the mainland dispatched the schooner Peores [Peor es] Nada to the island to bring away the remaining inhabitants, the vessel sailing about the 1st of August, 1835.

After the Indian women were collected on the beach ready for embarking one of them made signs that her child had been left behind, and she was permitted to go in search of it. While she was absent a violent wind suddenly arose and they were compelled to set sail and leave the unfortunate woman and her child behind. The schooner ran before the wind to San Pedro, where the Indians were landed, and later it was wrecked in the attempt to enter the Golden Gate. There being no other craft larger than Indian canoes on this portion of the coast, no effort was made to rescue the woman, and the fact of her abandonment gradually passed out of mind.

In July 1853, Captain Nidever, of Santa Barbara, having learned the story of the abandoned woman, set sail for San Nicolas with the avowed intention of finding the woman. His crew consisted of Charles Brown, an Irishman who name has not been preserved, and four Mission Indians. The woman was finally found in a little wickiup, or circular inclosure, made of brush. When the men came upon her she was removing the blubber from a seal. She arose with dignity, saluting the strangers with a smile. She was dressed in a gown made of bird skins, which was was cut low in the neck and reached to her ankles. Her hair was a yellowish brown, probably owing in part to exposure, and she appeared to be about 50 years of age. After roasting some nuts for her guests, of which they partook with a relish, they made signs for her to accompany them to the vessel, which she did without hesitation. The woman gave evidence of having seen much suffering during the 18 years of her isolation, but she was very cheerful and remained so to the last.
On reaching Santa Barbara the rescued castaway became an object of great interest.

Gonzales, Sanchez and Jimeno—came to see her, and although they were acquainted with many dialects yet they could not understand hers, nor could she understand them. Indians were brought from Santa Ynez, Los Angeles and other places, but none were able to converse with her.

p.span: It is probable that she had entirely forgotten her own dialect and during the 18 weary years of her solitude had constructed another known only to herself.

She soon became quite adept in conversing by signs, and gave Captain Nidever to understand that when she went back for her child she found that it had been killed and devoured by dogs, which had so prostrated her that she had no heart to return to the ship. She had sometimes seen ships sailing past the island, but none ever came to rescue her.

div.p: The food of civilization, of which she partook to excess, did not agree with her, and she died about four weeks after landing at Santa Barbara. After her death her gown of birdskins and various trinkets were sent to the Vatican at Rome.

San Nicolas has proved to be a veritable treasure house for the archaeologist and relic hunter. Tons of relics of the aboriginal inhabitants have been unearthed and carried away—and still a small party from Santa Catalina Islands, consisting of A. B. Chappell, one of the characters of the island, better known as "Chappie," who was guide to the expedition, this being his fifth trip; William Garnish, of Ashland, Wis.; Mrs. L. E. Francher, of Mansfield, Ohio; Mrs. Martha Dickinson-Hammond, and Hon. George C. Thaxter, of Redlands—chartered the schooner Nellie and proceeded to San Nicolas in search of adventure and curios. The Captain landed the party and then sailed away, promising to return at the end of one month and pick up the marooned relic hunters.

p.span: It was a custom with the Indians, at the death of one of their tribe, to bury with him for ready use in the happy hunting grounds

Hence it is necessary to find their burial places to find their treasures. These burial places were nearly always in the vicinity of the waters, and the skeletons are found invariably interred in a sitting posture, with the arms clasped about the knees.

The fact that the Indians of San Nicolas buried their dead in coffins is made the basis for the claim that they attained to a higher state of civilization than those of the other islands or of the mainland, as no other tribes have been known to use coffins.

div.p: It appears also that caste was observed among them, for all were not thus buried. The coffins frequently yielded a rich harvest, and the articles found in them were almost invariably perfectly preserved. The coffins were neatly constructed of plank from an inch to an inch and a half thick, but from what source it was derived furnishes another opportunity for conjecture. They were cemented together, made waterproof and airtight with asphaltum, and usually measured about two feet square and four feet long. They contained, with one exception, a single skeleton each. The exception contained three. One of the skulls was an enormous one, measuring 22 inches in circumference, with an extraordinary high forehead and cranial development indicating a high order of intellect. The other two were much smaller, giving occasion for the belief that they might have been a chief and his two wives. That one was a chief of importance is shown from the fact that a fine pestle taken from this coffin was found to be inlaid with a shell in the handle.

When opened the hair on one of the skulls was apparently in a per fect [sic] state of preservation, but as soon as the air was admitted it fell into impalpable powder, while the odor was so overpowering that it was two days before the contents of the coffin could be examined.

span.span: Invariably the relic hunter came to know when he was coming upon a coffin, for he always found the skeleton of a dog, with a shell fishhook or earring, buried about 18 inches above the coffin.

It seems evident from appearances that the bodies were not placed in the coffins immediately at death, but were left to decay to some extent, at least, for it was impossible that the three bodies the skeletons of which were found in a single coffin could have been placed in so small a space.
Were also marked with headstones or monuments of whalebones, usually monstrous ribs or large sections of the vertebrae.

The treasure boxes found in the coffins consist of two abalone shells, one inverted over the other, the upper one being the smaller, so as to fit inside the lower, and the two are then tightly plastered together with asphaltum. Those opened contained pearls, earrings, fishhooks, whistles, bracelets, wampum, &c. The usual place for finding wampums, however, is in the mouth of the cadaver.

Many of the skulls when found are encased in shells deftly placed all over and about them. One skull taken out had a wicker basket inverted over it, which some solicitous friend thought to preserve to its owner in the spirit world by pouring asphaltum into it and clapping it on the head. They neglected to coat it on the outside, however, and when found the only trace of the basket was its outline in the asphaltum.

Enormous shell mounds are found in all parts of the island, and are more numerous as the interior is reached, where, though miles from the water's edge, the whole surface of the ground is seemingly covered with shells, presenting a picture of unparalleled brillance in the sunlight. Many of these shells are yet whole and of most extraordinary size. The coloring is exquisite, the garishness having been softened only by age, many of them, no doubt, being centuries old.

div.p: The island has but one human inhabitant, the lone shepherd of San Nicolas. His is not an enviable lot, although he seems to enjoy his isolation. A few sheep are kept on the island, though in the absence of bushes and grass one wonders how they exist. Their principal food is a thick-leaved, waxy "ice" plant, which grows out of the sand at the eastern end of the island. The sheperd's sole companion is his dog.

The old stone hut at the western end of the island, put up by Chinese fishermen many years ago, aptly illustrates the general character of the weather of San Nicolas. There is no harbor on the island, and on account of the violent winds which come up without a moment's notice it is unsafe for vessels to lie at anchor there. The hut—about 15 by 30 feet in size, and built with heavy walls of stone—originally on the level surface, has now the appearance of a Kansas dug-out. The winds have heaped the sands about it until only the roof is visible above the surrounding level.
